source_text: We speak Italian at home.

reference_translations.notes: Subject pronoun dropped, the natural default.
Subject pronoun noi included for emphasis.

explanation: Parlare is a regular -are verb; the noi form is parl- + -iamo: parliamo. Italian normally drops the subject pronoun, so 'parliamo' alone already means 'we speak'.

source_text: The children sing a song in the garden.

reference_translations.text: I bambini cantano una canzone in giardino.
reference_translations.notes: Standard.

reference_translations.text: I bambini cantono una canzone in giardino.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: -are takes -ano, not -ono, in the 3pl.

explanation: Cantare is a regular -are verb; the loro form takes -ano: cantano. The cross-class slip cantono borrows the -ere/-ire ending and is wrong here.

source_text: They take the train every morning.

reference_translations.notes: Standard; subject pronoun dropped.
WRONG: -ere 3pl is -ono, not -ano.

explanation: Prendere is a regular -ere verb; its loro form is prend- + -ono: prendono. Using prendano wrongly borrows the first-conjugation -ano.

source_text: We write a letter to our grandparents.

reference_translations.notes: Standard; a + i = ai.

reference_translations.text: Noi scriviamo una lettera ai nostri nonni.
reference_translations.notes: With possessive and subject pronoun, equally correct.

explanation: Scrivere is a regular -ere verb; the noi form is the shared -iamo ending: scriviamo. The -iamo ending is the same across all three classes.

source_text: I sleep eight hours a night.

reference_translations.notes: Standard.
WRONG: dormire is a plain -ire verb and takes no -isc-.

explanation: Dormire is a plain third-conjugation verb, so the io form is just dorm- + -o: dormo. Only the -isc- pattern adds -isc-, and dormire is not in it.

source_text: I don't understand this word.

reference_translations.notes: Standard; -isc- in the io form.
WRONG: capire takes the -isc- insert in the io form.

explanation: Capire is an -isc- verb, so the io form inserts -isc-: cap- + -isc- + -o gives capisco. The insert appears in the io / tu / lui / loro forms.

source_text: We finish work at six.

reference_translations.notes: Standard; NO -isc- in the noi form.
WRONG: the -isc- pattern skips the noi form.

explanation: Finire is an -isc- verb, but the insert never appears in the noi form: fin- + -iamo gives finiamo, not finisciamo. The pattern is boot-shaped, skipping noi and voi.

source_text: They are building a new house in the countryside.

reference_translations.text: Costruiscono una nuova casa in campagna.
reference_translations.notes: English progressive maps to the plain Italian present.

reference_translations.text: Stanno costruendo una nuova casa in campagna.
reference_translations.notes: Progressive with stare + gerundio; also correct but not required here.

explanation: Costruire is an -isc- verb; the loro form is costru- + -isc- + -ono: costruiscono. Italian usually renders the English progressive 'are building' with the plain present.

source_text: I am a teacher and my sister is a student.

reference_translations.text: Sono un'insegnante e mia sorella è una studentessa.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; note the accent on è.

reference_translations.text: Sono un'insegnante e mia sorella e una studentessa.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: 'e' without the accent is 'and', not the verb 'is'.

explanation: Essere is suppletive: the io form is sono and the lui / lei form is è, with a written accent. The accent distinguishes è (is) from e (and), two different words.

source_text: We are old friends.

reference_translations.notes: Standard.
With subject pronoun, equally correct.

explanation: The noi form of essere is siamo, a suppletive shape unrelated to the singular sono or sei. It is held in memory as its own word.

source_text: They have two children and a dog.

reference_translations.text: Hanno due figli e un cane.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; the silent h is obligatory.

reference_translations.text: Anno due figli e un cane.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: without the h, 'anno' is the noun 'year'.

explanation: The loro form of avere is hanno, with a silent but obligatory h. Drop it and 'anno' means 'year', a different word.

source_text: I'm hungry and you're thirsty.

reference_translations.text: Ho fame e tu hai sete.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; Italian uses avere for hunger and thirst.

reference_translations.text: O fame e tu ai sete.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: both forms drop the obligatory silent h.

translation.reveal: hunger - fame (avere fame = to be hungry)
thirst - sete (avere sete = to be thirsty)

explanation: Italian expresses hunger and thirst with avere: ho fame (io) and hai sete (tu). Both forms carry the silent h that marks them off from 'o' (or) and 'ai' (to the).

source_text: We're really hungry this evening.

reference_translations.notes: Standard; the noi form has no h.
WRONG: the noi form abbiamo never takes an h.

translation.reveal: hunger - fame (avere fame = to be hungry)

explanation: The noi form of avere is abbiamo, with no h: the silent h belongs only to ho, hai, ha and hanno, the four forms that would otherwise collide with another word.

source_text: Every day I go to work by bike.

reference_translations.text: Ogni giorno vado al lavoro in bici.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; vado is the contracted io form.

reference_translations.text: Ogni giorno ando al lavoro in bici.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: andare has no regular 'ando'; the io form is vado.

explanation: Andare has a contracted, irregular present: the io form is vado, not the regular-looking ando. These high-frequency verbs are learned as whole paradigms.

source_text: She gives me a hand every day.

reference_translations.text: Mi dà una mano ogni giorno.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; note the accent on dà.

reference_translations.text: Mi da una mano ogni giorno.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: without the accent, 'da' is the preposition 'from'.

translation.reveal: hand - mano (dare una mano = to lend a hand)

explanation: The lui / lei form of dare is dà, with a written accent that keeps it apart from da, the preposition 'from'.

source_text: We do the shopping on Saturday.

reference_translations.notes: Standard; 'fare la spesa' = to do the grocery shopping.

reference_translations.text: Noi facciamo la spesa il sabato.
reference_translations.notes: With subject pronoun, equally correct.

explanation: The noi form of fare is facciamo, built on the older fac- stem with a double c. Italian says 'fare la spesa' for doing the grocery shopping.

source_text: I want a coffee, please.

reference_translations.notes: Standard; voglio + noun.
Conditional 'I would like' is more polite but is a different tense, not the plain present.

explanation: The io form of volere is voglio. Unlike the other modals, volere takes a noun directly: voglio un caffè. The polite 'vorrei' is the conditional, a separate form.

source_text: Can you come with us tonight?

reference_translations.notes: Standard; puoi + infinitive.
WRONG: 'poi' is the adverb 'then'; the verb is puoi.

translation.reveal: to be able to / can - potere

explanation: The tu form of potere is puoi, with the diphthong uo, followed straight by an infinitive: puoi venire. The look-alike 'poi' means 'then'.

source_text: We have to leave early tomorrow.

reference_translations.notes: Standard; dobbiamo + infinitive.
With subject pronoun, equally correct.

explanation: The noi form of dovere is dobbiamo, with a double b and an o for the infinitive's u. A modal leads into an infinitive: dobbiamo partire.

source_text: They want to go to the seaside in August.

reference_translations.text: Vogliono andare al mare ad agosto.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; a + il = al, and 'ad' before a vowel.

reference_translations.text: Volono andare al mare ad agosto.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: the loro form keeps the gli of voglio: vogliono.

explanation: The loro form of volere is vogliono, keeping the gli sound of the singular voglio. The regular-looking volono is not a form.

source_text: I always tell the truth.

reference_translations.notes: Standard; io form on the dic- stem.
WRONG: the io form restores the dic- stem: dico.

explanation: Dire restores the older dic- stem in the present: the io form is dic- + -o, dico. The contracted infinitive hides that stem.

source_text: In the morning I drink a coffee and read the newspaper.

reference_translations.text: La mattina bevo un caffè e leggo il giornale.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; bere opens out to bev-.

reference_translations.text: La mattina bero un caffè e leggo il giornale.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: bere conjugates on the bev- stem: bevo.

explanation: Bere opens out to the older stem bev- in the present: the io form is bev- + -o, bevo. Leggere alongside it is a plain -ere verb (leggo).

source_text: I come from Spain but I live in Rome.

reference_translations.text: Vengo dalla Spagna ma vivo a Roma.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; vengo has the -g- insert, da + la = dalla.

reference_translations.text: Veno dalla Spagna ma vivo a Roma.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: venire inserts -g- in the io form: vengo.

explanation: Venire inserts a -g- in the io form: ven- + g + -o gives vengo. Vivere beside it is a plain -ere verb (vivo).

source_text: We usually come on foot.

reference_translations.notes: Standard; the noi form has no -g-.
WRONG: the -g- never appears in the noi form: veniamo.

explanation: The -g- of venire appears only in the io and loro forms, never in noi: ven- + -iamo gives veniamo. Vengiamo over-extends the insert.

source_text: In the evening I go out with friends.

reference_translations.text: La sera esco con gli amici.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; uscire shifts u to e under stress.

reference_translations.text: La sera uscio con gli amici.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: the io form shifts the stem vowel: esco.

explanation: Uscire changes its stressed stem vowel from u to e in the singular and loro: the io form is esco, not uscio.

source_text: We go out at eight every evening.

reference_translations.notes: Standard; the noi form keeps the plain u-stem.
WRONG: the e-stem reverts to u in the noi form: usciamo.

explanation: In the noi form the stress moves to the ending and the stem reverts to u: usc- + -iamo gives usciamo. Keeping the e-stem (esciamo) is the common slip.

source_text: You always look for your keys in the morning.

reference_translations.text: Cerchi sempre le chiavi la mattina.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; the h keeps the c hard before -i.

reference_translations.text: Ceri sempre le chiavi la mattina.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: -care verbs add an h before the -i ending: cerchi.

explanation: Cercare adds an h before the tu ending to keep the c hard: cerc- + h + -i gives cerchi. Without the h, ceri would be read with a soft c.

source_text: We eat at eight in the evening.

reference_translations.notes: Standard; the stem i drops before the i-initial ending.
WRONG: -giare verbs drop the stem i before -iamo: mangiamo.

explanation: Mangiare drops its stem i before the i-initial noi ending: mang- + -iamo gives mangiamo. One i both softens the g and forms the ending.

source_text: You send a lot of emails every day.

reference_translations.notes: Standard; the stressed stem i is kept, giving a double i.
WRONG: inviare stresses its stem i, so it is kept: invii.

explanation: Inviare stresses its stem i (in-VI-o), so it is kept and the tu ending adds another: invii. Reducing it to invi would drop the stressed vowel.

source_text: My name is Anna and I get up early.

reference_translations.text: Mi chiamo Anna e mi alzo presto.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; reflexive pronoun mi for io.
WRONG: both verbs are reflexive and need the pronoun mi.

explanation: Both verbs are reflexive, so each needs the io pronoun mi before it: mi chiamo, mi alzo. Dropping the pronoun changes the meaning.

source_text: The children wash their hands before dinner.

reference_translations.text: I bambini si lavano le mani prima di cena.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; reflexive si with the -ano form.

reference_translations.text: I bambini lavano le mani prima di cena.
reference_translations.notes: Without si this means they wash someone else's hands, not their own.

explanation: For loro the reflexive pronoun is si, paired with the -ano verb form: si lavano. The pronoun marks the washing as turning back on the subject (their own hands).

source_text: We get up early every day.

reference_translations.notes: Standard; reflexive ci with the -iamo form.
WRONG: the noi reflexive pronoun is ci, not si.

explanation: For noi the reflexive pronoun is ci, paired with the -iamo ending: ci alziamo. The pronoun and the verb ending both carry the 'we' meaning and must match.
